summary: Matched and migrated a large Donkey Kong fighter assembly slice from asm/melee/ft/chara/ftdonkey_1.s into C sources under src/melee/ft/chara/ftDonkey. The PR deleted the 1276-line ftdonkey_1.s file, added/expanded many numbered ftdonkey C files, wired them into obj_files.mk, renamed many anonymous func_800D*/func_800E* symbols to ftDonkey_* names in action tables and other asm callsites, and added fighter/header fields needed to express Donkey Kong special-move state. The largest visible C addition is ftdonkey13.c, implementing SpecialN/Giant Punch behavior including charge counts, callbacks, hitlag effect pause/resume, ground/air action transitions, and effect cleanup. Evidence is strong for broad DK matching and symbol migration, but there were no PR comments or reviews to capture reviewer rationale.
what_changed: Deleted asm/melee/ft/chara/ftdonkey_1.s, removing 1276 lines of PowerPC assembly for functions originally named func_800DF938 through the surrounding ftDonkey code range.

Added C implementations across numbered ftDonkey files, with ftdonkey13.c alone adding 479 lines for Donkey Kong SpecialN/Giant Punch start actions, charge handling, callbacks, effects, ground/air transitions, and cleanup.

Added ftdonkey14.c with SpecialHi/Spinning Kong-related functions such as ftDonkey_SetCallbacks_SpecialHi, ftDonkey_SpecialHi_StartAction, ftDonkey_SpecialAirHi_StartAction, and follow-up callbacks at ftDonkey_8010FD10/8010FDA4/8010FE60.
Updated obj_files.mk to replace asm/melee/ft/chara/ftdonkey_1.s.o with src/melee/ft/chara/ftDonkey/ftdonkey1.c.o through ftdonkey14.c.o while retaining ftdonkey_2.s.o.

Updated action-state tables and asm callsites to use ftDonkey_* symbol names instead of generic func_800D*/func_800E* names, for example ftDonkey_800DF9F0, ftDonkey_800DFA50, ftDonkey_800DFB34, ftDonkey_800DFCA4, ftDonkey_800DFDDC, ftDonkey_800DFEC8, ftDonkey_800E0060, and ftDonkey_GetWalkType_800DFBF8.
Changed external references in other asm files, including code_800877F8.s, ftkirby.s, and ftmario.s, so callers branch to the newly named ftDonkey functions.
Expanded ftdonkey.h with ftDonkeyAttributes corrections/prototypes and declarations for newly C-matched ftDonkey routines.

Extended fighter-related state usage with fields such as sa.dk.x222C, x2340_stateVar1, x2344_stateVar2, x2348_stateVar3, x234C_stateVar4, x2350_stateVar5, x2354_stateVar6, x2358_stateVar7, x235C_f32, and x2360_f32 as needed by matched DK routines.
Reworked include/functions.h extensively, apparently to add/update prototypes after converting and renaming the Donkey Kong symbols.
smart_moves: Converted the DK assembly slice as a module rather than in isolation: the PR updated build inputs, headers, action tables, and all known external asm callers so the new C symbols linked consistently.
Kept address-based names for uncertain functions while adding a meaningful name where behavior was clear, notably ftDonkey_GetWalkType_800DFBF8 for the walk-speed bucket helper.
Centralized repeated SpecialN callback setup in ftDonkey_SetCallbacks_SpecialN, assigning OnTakeDamage, OnDeath2, effect-destroy, EnterHitlag, and ExitHitlag callbacks in one place.
Added helper cleanup routines ftDonkey_NullCallbacks, ftDonkey_DestroyAllEffects, and ftDonkey_DestroyAllEffectsPlus to mirror repeated callback/effect teardown patterns.
Used fighter special attributes via getFtSpecialAttrs/getFtSpecialAttrs2CC/COPY_ATTRS/PUSH_ATTRS rather than scattering raw ext_attr access everywhere.

Preserved matching-sensitive constructs visible in the diff, including unused stack arrays and comments such as 'getFighter inlines not working' / 'get inline break the regalloc', indicating deliberate register-allocation control.
Introduced an inline-like duplicate ftDonkey_GetWalkType_800DFBF8_fake to support callers that needed the same logic but different inlining/register allocation for matching.
decomp_lessons: When deleting a large asm file, update every cross-reference immediately: action-state tables and unrelated asm callsites may still branch to old func_* labels.
For fighter character work, matching often requires naming and modeling per-character state variables in Fighter, not only translating the local function body.
Callback setup and teardown are good decomp anchors: repeated writes to fighter->cb fields can reveal move lifecycle structure and justify helper functions.

Action-state IDs and transition flags should be preserved exactly during migration; this PR uses raw IDs such as 0x171 through 0x17A and flag constants like 0x0C4C5080/0x0C4D508E where semantic enum names were not yet present.

Do not over-name uncertain functions. The PR mostly keeps address-derived ftDonkey_8010xxxx/ftDonkey_800Dxxxx names where behavior is not obvious, while giving clearer names to visible helpers such as SpecialN_StartAction, SpecialAirN_StartAction, SpecialHi_StartAction, and GetWalkType.
Inline helper variants can be a practical matching tactic when the same source-level logic must compile differently in different call contexts.

Fighter special move decomp should track both command variables, such as x2200_ftcmd_var0 through x220C_ftcmd_var3, and state variables, such as x2340/x2344/x2348/x234C, because animation callbacks and transition code both depend on them.
naming_conventions: Use the character prefix ftDonkey_ for Donkey Kong-specific functions after migration from generic func_* labels.
Use action-oriented names for confident entry points, e.g. ftDonkey_SpecialN_StartAction, ftDonkey_SpecialAirN_StartAction, ftDonkey_SpecialHi_StartAction, ftDonkey_SpecialAirHi_StartAction.
Use helper names that describe lifecycle behavior when clear, e.g. ftDonkey_SetCallbacks_SpecialN, ftDonkey_SetCallbacks_SpecialHi, ftDonkey_NullCallbacks, ftDonkey_DestroyAllEffects.
Keep address-suffixed names such as ftDonkey_8010E930 or ftDonkey_800DFCA4 when the exact semantic name is not established.
Use field suffixes to record inferred type/use on ambiguous Fighter state fields, e.g. x2348_stateVar3_s32, x2354_stateVar6_f32, x2360_f32.
Use a descriptive helper name with the original address retained when behavior is known but address identity still matters, e.g. ftDonkey_GetWalkType_800DFBF8.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Deleted matched assembly only after adding C object files and updating obj_files.mk, preventing duplicate symbols or missing object coverage.
Renamed .4byte action-state callback entries from generic function names to ftDonkey_* symbols so table data continued to point at the matched C functions.

Updated branch targets in remaining assembly to the new symbols, e.g. calls to func_800DFCA4 became ftDonkey_800DFCA4, func_800DFDDC became ftDonkey_800DFDDC, and func_800DFBF8 became ftDonkey_GetWalkType_800DFBF8.
Used small wrapper functions for one-call behavior, such as functions that just call func_80084F3C, func_80084EEC, or transition helpers, matching the short asm thunks from the deleted file.
Used explicit unused locals/arrays to stabilize stack layout and register allocation in functions where getFighter/getFighterPlus inlining changed codegen.
Used a duplicate inline-style walk-type helper to reproduce codegen around fabs_inline, p_ftCommonData thresholds, xEC_ground_vel, x118_WalkMaximumVelocity, and x2360_f32.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that rename ftDonkey_8010xxxx or ftDonkey_800Dxxxx functions from this PR to semantic names.
Check whether ftDonkey_GetWalkType_800DFBF8_fake was later removed or replaced once matching constraints changed.
Search follow-up changes to Fighter fields x2340 through x2360 to see which Donkey Kong state variables received stable names or struct documentation.
Inspect later PRs touching ftdonkey13.c and ftdonkey14.c for cleanup of raw action-state IDs 0x171-0x17E into enums.
Look for later header refactors that reduce include/functions.h churn caused by per-character decomp migrations.
